title: The Canvas & The Game Loop
html:
Welcome to your chosen path

You finished Foundations, and you have chosen JavaScript with HTML5 Canvas as your single track. Great choice. This path stands completely on its own: by the end you will have built a small portfolio of real games, FROM SCRATCH, with no engine, no install, and no build step. Just a browser and a text file.

Here is the arc you will build, one game per lesson, each teaching something new:

  • Toy — a bouncing box on a canvas (this lesson)
  • Pong — the original arcade classic (input & movement)
  • Breakout — bricks, paddle, collision (AABB)
  • Flappy-style tapper — a NEW genre (gravity, tap input)
  • Platformer — a NEW genre (gravity + jumping + tiles)
  • Top-down shooter — a NEW genre (bullets, spawning, vectors)
  • Match puzzle — a NEW genre (grids, matching, state)
  • Capstone — a polished arcade game, DEPLOYED to the web

Several distinct genres, all in one language. Let us begin.

One self-contained page

A canvas game is just an HTML page. No frameworks. Create a file called index.html, paste this, and double-click it. It runs.

index.html
<!-- The whole game lives in one file --> <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head><meta charset='utf-8'><title>My Game</title></head> <body style='margin:0;background:#111'> <canvas id='game' width='480' height='320'></canvas> <script> // all our game code goes here </script> </body> </html>

The <canvas> is a rectangle of pixels you draw onto with JavaScript. To draw, grab the element and ask it for a 2D context — the object with all the drawing methods.

main.js
const canvas = document.getElementById('game'); const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); ctx.fillStyle = '#3cf'; // set color ctx.fillRect(40, 40, 80, 60); // x, y, width, height

That draws one cyan rectangle. The origin (0,0) is the top-left; x grows right, y grows down. Remember that — y-down trips up everyone at first.

The game loop & delta time

A game is the same three steps, sixty times a second: update the world, clear the screen, draw it again. The browser gives us requestAnimationFrame (rAF) to sync that to the monitor's refresh.

Crucially, frames are not perfectly even. So we never move things by a fixed amount per frame — we multiply by delta time (dt), the seconds elapsed since the last frame. Speeds become "pixels per second" and your game runs the same on a 60Hz or 144Hz screen.

loop.js
let last = 0; function frame(now) { const dt = Math.min((now - last) / 1000, 0.05); // ms -> secs, clamped last = now; update(dt); render(); requestAnimationFrame(frame); // ask for the next frame } requestAnimationFrame(frame); // kick it off
Tip.
The first dt is weird because last starts at 0. The Math.min clamp caps it so a paused tab does not teleport everything off-screen when you return.

A box that moves

Give the box a position and a velocity (pixels per second) and move it with dt.

box.js
const box = { x: 40, y: 40, vx: 120, vy: 90, s: 30 }; function update(dt) { box.x += box.vx * dt; // move 120 px every second box.y += box.vy * dt; } function render() { ctx.fillStyle = '#111'; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height); // clear ctx.fillStyle = '#3cf'; ctx.fillRect(box.x, box.y, box.s, box.s); }

Run it: the box slides off-screen forever. Next lesson it will bounce — but first, your job.

Lab.
Open the page, then change vx and vy. Comment out the clear line in render and watch the box smear into a trail — proof the loop redraws every frame.
🎮 Build This: Toy — Bouncing Box

Genre: Toy. This STARTS the arc — your very first canvas program, the seed every later game grows from.

  1. Create index.html with a 480x320 canvas and a <script>.
  2. Grab the context and set up the rAF loop with clamped dt.
  3. Give the box x, y, vx, vy and move it in update(dt).
  4. Make it BOUNCE: if box.x < 0 or box.x + box.s > canvas.width, flip box.vx = -box.vx. Same for y with vy.
  5. Bonus: change the fill color each time it bounces.

title: Keyboard, Mouse, Touch — and Pong
html:
Games listen for input

Last lesson the box moved on its own. A game responds to a player. The browser fires events for keys, mouse, and touch. The trick: do not act inside the event itself — instead record input in a state object that update(dt) reads every frame. This keeps movement smooth and tied to delta time.

Keyboard

Track which keys are currently held in a plain object keyed by event.code.

keys.js
const keys = {}; addEventListener('keydown', e => { keys[e.code] = true; }); addEventListener('keyup', e => { keys[e.code] = false; }); // in update(dt): if (keys['ArrowUp']) player.y -= speed * dt; if (keys['ArrowDown']) player.y += speed * dt;

e.code gives physical key names like 'KeyW', 'Space', 'ArrowLeft' — independent of keyboard layout. Use it, not e.key, for movement.

Mouse & touch

For pointer position you must convert from page coordinates to canvas coordinates using the canvas's bounding box. Touch is almost identical — a touch carries the same clientX/clientY.

pointer.js
const mouse = { x: 0, y: 0 }; function toCanvas(clientX, clientY) { const r = canvas.getBoundingClientRect(); mouse.x = clientX - r.left; mouse.y = clientY - r.top; } canvas.addEventListener('mousemove', e => toCanvas(e.clientX, e.clientY)); canvas.addEventListener('touchmove', e => { e.preventDefault(); // stop the page scrolling const t = e.touches[0]; toCanvas(t.clientX, t.clientY); }, { passive: false });
Tip.
preventDefault() on touch stops the browser scrolling or zooming while the player drags. Pass { passive: false } or the browser ignores your preventDefault.

Building Pong

Pong is two paddles, a ball, and scores. We reuse everything from lesson 1: a velocity-driven ball that bounces, plus paddles driven by input. Here is the left paddle (W/S keys) and a mouse/touch-driven right paddle.

pong.js
const W = canvas.width, H = canvas.height; const ball = { x: W/2, y: H/2, vx: 200, vy: 140, r: 7 }; const left = { x: 20, y: H/2-30, w: 10, h: 60 }; const right = { x: W-30, y: H/2-30, w: 10, h: 60 }; const PADDLE = 260; // px/sec function update(dt) { if (keys['KeyW']) left.y -= PADDLE * dt; if (keys['KeyS']) left.y += PADDLE * dt; right.y = mouse.y - right.h/2; // follow pointer ball.x += ball.vx * dt; ball.y += ball.vy * dt; if (ball.y < 0 || ball.y > H) ball.vy *= -1; // top/bottom }

To bounce off a paddle, check overlap between the ball and the paddle rectangle, then reverse vx. (We will formalize this rectangle test next lesson; for now an inline check is fine.)

paddle-hit.js
function hits(p) { return ball.x > p.x && ball.x < p.x+p.w && ball.y > p.y && ball.y < p.y+p.h; } if (hits(left) || hits(right)) { ball.vx *= -1.05; // reverse + speed up a touch }

Render is just rectangles and a circle (ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(...); ctx.fill();). Add a score: increment when the ball passes an edge, then reset the ball to center.

Lab.
Clamp the paddles inside the field: left.y = Math.max(0, Math.min(H - left.h, left.y)); so they cannot slide off the top or bottom.
🎮 Build This: Arcade — Pong

Genre: Arcade. This STARTS the arcade genre, building directly on the bouncing-box loop from Lesson 1.

  1. Set up the loop and the keys + mouse/touch trackers.
  2. Left paddle on W/S; right paddle follows the mouse OR touch.
  3. Ball bounces off top and bottom; reverses (and speeds up) on paddle hits.
  4. When the ball exits left or right, award a point to the other side and reset the ball to center with a random vy.
  5. Draw the two scores with ctx.fillText. Bonus: a dashed center net.

title: Collision Detection & Breakout
html:
The most useful trick in 2D games

Pong's paddle check was a one-off. Now we make it reusable. The workhorse of 2D collision is AABB — Axis-Aligned Bounding Box. Two rectangles overlap if they overlap on BOTH the x-axis and the y-axis. One tiny function powers Breakout, platformers, shooters — almost everything.

aabb.js
function aabb(a, b) { return a.x < b.x + b.w && a.x + a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y + b.h && a.y + a.h > b.y; } // each object just needs x, y, w, h

Read it as: "a's left edge is left of b's right edge, AND a's right edge is right of b's left edge" — repeated for the vertical axis. All four true means the boxes touch.

Tip.
A ball is round, but treating it as a small square for AABB is fine for Breakout and most arcade games. A true circle-vs-rect test clamps the circle center to the rectangle and measures distance — overkill here.

Which side did we hit?

Knowing you collided is not enough — you need to know whether to flip vx or vy. A simple, reliable approach: compare the ball-to-rect offset on each axis (normalized by half-size) and bounce off the dominant axis.

resolve.js
function bounceOff(ball, rect) { const bx = ball.x + ball.w/2, by = ball.y + ball.h/2; const rx = rect.x + rect.w/2, ry = rect.y + rect.h/2; const dx = (bx - rx) / (rect.w/2); const dy = (by - ry) / (rect.h/2); if (Math.abs(dx) > Math.abs(dy)) ball.vx = Math.sign(dx) * Math.abs(ball.vx); else ball.vy = Math.sign(dy) * Math.abs(ball.vy); }

Breakout: the brick grid

Breakout is Pong with a twist: instead of an opponent, a wall of bricks. Generate the grid as an array of rectangles, each with an alive flag.

bricks.js
const bricks = []; const COLS = 8, ROWS = 4, BW = 54, BH = 18, PAD = 4; for (let r = 0; r < ROWS; r++) for (let c = 0; c < COLS; c++) bricks.push({ x: 20 + c*(BW+PAD), y: 30 + r*(BH+PAD), w: BW, h: BH, alive: true });

Each frame, after moving the ball, loop the bricks. On a hit, kill the brick, bounce, and add score. The paddle uses the same AABB.

update.js
function update(dt) { paddle.x = mouse.x - paddle.w/2; ball.x += ball.vx * dt; ball.y += ball.vy * dt; if (ball.x < 0 || ball.x+ball.w > W) ball.vx *= -1; if (ball.y < 0) ball.vy *= -1; if (aabb(ball, paddle)) bounceOff(ball, paddle); for (const b of bricks) { if (b.alive && aabb(ball, b)) { b.alive = false; score += 10; bounceOff(ball, b); break; // one brick per frame avoids double-bounces } } if (ball.y > H) { lives--; resetBall(); } // fell off bottom }

Render bricks with a quick loop, skipping dead ones. You now have a complete, satisfying arcade game.

Lab.
Make the bounce angle depend on WHERE the ball hits the paddle: const t = (ball.x - paddle.x) / paddle.w; ball.vx = (t - 0.5) * 400;. Now players can aim — the heart of Breakout's skill.
🎮 Build This: Arcade — Breakout

Genre: Arcade. This EXTENDS your Pong code — keep the loop, ball, and paddle; replace the second paddle with a brick wall.

  1. Add the aabb helper and the bounceOff resolver.
  2. Generate an 8x4 brick grid as an array of {x,y,w,h,alive}.
  3. Paddle follows mouse/touch; ball bounces off walls, paddle, and bricks.
  4. Kill bricks on hit, add score, lose a life when the ball drops off the bottom.
  5. Win when all bricks are dead; show "You Win!". Bonus: paddle-position aiming from the Lab.

title: Gravity, Tap Input & a Flappy Tapper
html:
A brand-new genre

Arcade bouncing is symmetric — energy in, energy out. Real-feeling movement needs acceleration: a force that constantly changes velocity. The most famous force is gravity, and the most famous one-button game built on it is the Flappy-style tapper. This lesson STARTS a new genre, and the physics here carry straight into next lesson's platformer.

The physics of gravity

Every frame, gravity adds to vertical velocity; velocity then moves the object. Both steps scale by dt. Gravity is just a constant acceleration in px/sec².

gravity.js
const GRAV = 1400; // px per second, per second const FLAP = -420; // upward velocity on a tap (y is down!) const bird = { x: 80, y: 150, vy: 0, r: 12 }; function update(dt) { bird.vy += GRAV * dt; // accelerate downward bird.y += bird.vy * dt; // integrate position }

That is the entire feel of the game. A tap sets vy to a fixed negative number — an instant upward kick that gravity immediately fights. The arc you feel is acceleration at work.

Tap input: one action, three devices

This genre needs a single "flap" action triggered by a key press, a click, or a touch. Wire all three to one function. Note we use keydown (a discrete press) rather than the held-key state from Pong.

tap.js
function flap() { bird.vy = FLAP; } addEventListener('keydown', e => { if (e.code === 'Space') flap(); }); canvas.addEventListener('mousedown', flap); canvas.addEventListener('touchstart', e => { e.preventDefault(); flap(); }, { passive: false });

Scrolling pipes

The bird stays put horizontally; the world scrolls left. Each pipe is a pair of rectangles with a gap. Spawn on a timer, move them left, remove off-screen ones.

pipes.js
let pipes = [], timer = 0; const GAP = 110, SPEED = 160, W = canvas.width, H = canvas.height; function spawnPipe() { const top = 40 + Math.random() * (H - GAP - 80); pipes.push({ x: W, top, bottom: top + GAP, w: 52, passed: false }); } function updatePipes(dt) { timer += dt; if (timer > 1.4) { timer = 0; spawnPipe(); } for (const p of pipes) p.x -= SPEED * dt; pipes = pipes.filter(p => p.x + p.w > 0); // drop off-screen pipes }

Collision & scoring

Reuse AABB from Breakout: treat the bird as a box, test it against the top and bottom pipe rects. Hitting either — or the ground/ceiling — ends the run. Score a point when a pipe's right edge passes the bird.

check.js
const b = { x: bird.x-bird.r, y: bird.y-bird.r, w: bird.r*2, h: bird.r*2 }; for (const p of pipes) { const topR = { x: p.x, y: 0, w: p.w, h: p.top }; const botR = { x: p.x, y: p.bottom, w: p.w, h: H - p.bottom }; if (aabb(b, topR) || aabb(b, botR)) gameOver(); if (!p.passed && p.x + p.w < bird.x) { p.passed = true; score++; } } if (bird.y > H || bird.y < 0) gameOver();
Tip.
Tune GRAV and FLAP together. High gravity with a strong flap feels twitchy; low values feel floaty. This pair IS your game's personality — spend time on it.
🎮 Build This: Tapper — Flappy Square

Genre: One-button tapper (NEW genre). This STARTS a new genre but reuses the aabb helper from your Breakout build.

  1. Bird with y, vy; gravity each frame; a tap sets vy = FLAP.
  2. Wire flap to Space, mouse, and touch.
  3. Spawn pipe pairs on a timer; scroll them left; remove off-screen ones.
  4. AABB the bird against both pipe rects and the screen edges; end the run on contact.
  5. Score when a pipe passes; show score and a "tap to restart" on game over. Bonus: a downward tilt based on vy using ctx.rotate.

title: Vectors, Tiles & a 2D Platformer
html:
A new genre: gravity meets ground

The Flappy tapper had gravity but nothing to stand on. A platformer adds solid ground and jumping — the player accelerates downward until landing on a platform, then can leap. This STARTS a new genre and leans on the gravity you learned last lesson.

Vectors, briefly

A vector is just an (x, y) pair representing a position, velocity, or direction. We have used them informally as {x, y} and {vx, vy}. Two operations matter most: adding vectors (apply velocity to position) and scaling (multiply by dt). That is the whole foundation of movement.

vec.js
// position += velocity * dt -- the universal movement step p.x += p.vx * dt; p.y += p.vy * dt;

The player & horizontal movement

Left/right is direct: held keys set horizontal velocity. We multiply by dt as always.

player.js
const p = { x: 40, y: 100, w: 22, h: 30, vx: 0, vy: 0, onGround: false }; const SPEED = 200, GRAV = 1500, JUMP = -520; function update(dt) { p.vx = 0; if (keys['ArrowLeft']) p.vx = -SPEED; if (keys['ArrowRight']) p.vx = SPEED; if (keys['Space'] && p.onGround) { p.vy = JUMP; p.onGround = false; } p.vy += GRAV * dt; move(dt); }

The platforms & landing

Platforms are just rectangles. The classic robust technique is axis-separated movement: move on x, resolve x collisions, THEN move on y, resolve y collisions. This prevents snagging on edges and makes "landing" reliable.

resolve.js
const platforms = [ { x: 0, y: 290, w: 480, h: 30 }, // floor { x: 120, y: 220, w: 100, h: 14 }, { x: 300, y: 170, w: 110, h: 14 } ]; function move(dt) { p.x += p.vx * dt; for (const t of platforms) if (aabb(p, t)) { if (p.vx > 0) p.x = t.x - p.w; else if (p.vx < 0) p.x = t.x + t.w; } p.y += p.vy * dt; p.onGround = false; for (const t of platforms) if (aabb(p, t)) { if (p.vy > 0) { p.y = t.y - p.h; p.onGround = true; } // landed else if (p.vy < 0) p.y = t.y + t.h; // bonked head p.vy = 0; } }

When falling (vy > 0) and overlapping a platform, snap the player to sit on top and flag onGround — exactly what the jump check reads. That single flag is the difference between a floaty mess and a real platformer.

Tip.
Add coyote time for game feel: allow a jump for ~0.1s AFTER leaving a ledge. Keep a coyote timer that resets to 0.1 when grounded and counts down otherwise; let the jump check use coyote > 0. Players will swear the controls feel "tight."

A camera (optional but easy)

For a level wider than the canvas, offset all drawing by a camera that follows the player. Before drawing, ctx.save(); ctx.translate(-camX, 0); then ctx.restore() after. The world scrolls; your collision code is unchanged.

🎮 Build This: Platformer — Square Quest

Genre: 2D platformer (NEW genre). This STARTS a new genre and reuses gravity from the Flappy tapper plus aabb from Breakout.

  1. Player with vx, vy, onGround; arrow keys move, Space jumps only when grounded.
  2. Apply gravity each frame; implement axis-separated movement and resolution.
  3. Lay out at least 4 platforms at different heights, including a floor.
  4. Add a few collectible coins (AABB pickups) and a goal flag that wins the level.
  5. Bonus: add coyote time and a camera that follows the player across a wide level.

title: Images, Spritesheet Animation & a Top-Down Shooter
html:
From rectangles to real art

Every game so far used fillRect. Now we draw images and animate spritesheets, then build a top-down shooter that spawns enemies and fires bullets using vectors. This STARTS a new genre.

Loading and drawing an image

Create an Image, set its src, and wait for onload before drawing. Use simple drawImage(img, x, y), or the scaled form with width and height.

image.js
const ship = new Image(); ship.src = 'ship.png'; let ready = false; ship.onload = () => { ready = true; }; // in render, once ready: ctx.drawImage(ship, player.x, player.y, 32, 32);
Tip.
Pixel art looks blurry when scaled. Turn off smoothing once: ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false;. Also add image-rendering:pixelated to the canvas style for crisp upscaled pixels.

Spritesheet animation

A spritesheet packs many frames into one image. Animation = drawing a different source rectangle each frame. The 9-argument drawImage cuts a sub-rectangle from the source and paints it to a destination rectangle.

anim.js
// drawImage(img, sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh) const FW = 16, FH = 16, FRAMES = 4; let frame = 0, animTimer = 0; function animate(dt) { animTimer += dt; if (animTimer > 0.12) { animTimer = 0; frame = (frame + 1) % FRAMES; } } function drawSprite(img, row, x, y) { ctx.drawImage(img, frame*FW, row*FH, FW, FH, x, y, FW*2, FH*2); }

The frame picks the column; a row argument picks the animation (walk, idle, etc.). Advancing on a timer — not every frame — keeps animation speed independent of frame rate.

The shooter: vectors for aiming

Top-down means no gravity; the player moves freely with WASD. To fire toward the mouse, build a direction vector from player to cursor and normalize it (scale to length 1) so bullet speed is constant in every direction.

shoot.js
const bullets = []; function fire() { let dx = mouse.x - player.x, dy = mouse.y - player.y; const len = Math.hypot(dx, dy) || 1; // vector length dx /= len; dy /= len; // normalize bullets.push({ x: player.x, y: player.y, vx: dx*500, vy: dy*500, w: 6, h: 6 }); } canvas.addEventListener('mousedown', fire);

Spawning enemies & hits

Enemies spawn at the edges and steer toward the player using the same normalize trick. Each frame: move bullets, move enemies, then check every bullet against every enemy with AABB; remove both on a hit.

enemies.js
function steer(e, dt) { let dx = player.x - e.x, dy = player.y - e.y; const len = Math.hypot(dx, dy) || 1; e.x += (dx/len) * 90 * dt; e.y += (dy/len) * 90 * dt; } // collision pass (loop backwards so splice is safe) for (let i = enemies.length-1; i >= 0; i--) for (let j = bullets.length-1; j >= 0; j--) if (aabb(enemies[i], bullets[j])) { enemies.splice(i, 1); bullets.splice(j, 1); score += 10; break; }
Tip.
No art? You can still ship: draw the player and enemies as colored circles or rotated triangles. Swap in spritesheet drawing later — your game logic does not change.
🎮 Build This: Shooter — Twin-Stick Survivor

Genre: Top-down shooter (NEW genre). This STARTS a new genre and reuses aabb, plus the normalize-vector aiming introduced here.

  1. WASD moves the player freely (no gravity); clamp to the screen.
  2. Click fires a bullet toward the mouse using a normalized direction vector.
  3. Spawn enemies at random edges on a timer; steer them toward the player.
  4. Bullet-vs-enemy AABB removes both and scores; enemy-vs-player ends the game.
  5. Bonus: load a spritesheet and animate the player and enemies; turn off image smoothing.

title: Web Audio, State Machines & a Match Puzzle
html:
Two missing pieces: sound and structure

You can make things move and collide. Two things separate a tech demo from a game: sound and a clear state machine (menu, playing, game over). We add both, then build a grid-based match puzzle — a brand-new genre with no physics at all. This STARTS a new genre.

Web Audio: sound with zero files

The Web Audio API can synthesize beeps and blips in code — perfect for prototypes. Create one AudioContext, then for each sound make an oscillator and a gain node and stop it after a moment.

sound.js
const audio = new (window.AudioContext || window.webkitAudioContext)(); function beep(freq, dur) { const osc = audio.createOscillator(); const gain = audio.createGain(); osc.frequency.value = freq; osc.type = 'square'; gain.gain.value = 0.08; // keep it gentle osc.connect(gain).connect(audio.destination); osc.start(); osc.stop(audio.currentTime + dur); // auto-stops } beep(660, 0.08); // a short click
Browser rule.
Browsers block audio until the user interacts. Call audio.resume() inside your first click or keydown handler, or sounds will be silent on page load. This is non-negotiable across all modern browsers.

To play a sound FILE instead, use an Audio element: const s = new Audio('hit.wav'); s.play();. For overlapping sounds, clone it: s.cloneNode().play();.

A state machine

Until now update always assumed "playing." Real games switch between screens. Model it as a single state string and branch on it in both update and render. This one pattern scales from Pong to your capstone.

state.js
let state = 'menu'; // 'menu' | 'play' | 'over' function update(dt) { if (state === 'play') updatePlay(dt); } function render() { if (state === 'menu') drawMenu(); else if (state === 'play') drawPlay(); else drawOver(); } addEventListener('keydown', e => { audio.resume(); if (state !== 'play' && e.code === 'Space') startGame(); });

The match puzzle: a grid of gems

No velocity here — just a 2D array of color indices. The player clicks two adjacent cells to swap; if the swap makes a line of three, those clear and the column refills. We store the grid and convert a click to grid coordinates.

grid.js
const N = 8, CELL = 40, COLORS = 5; const grid = []; for (let y = 0; y < N; y++) { grid[y] = []; for (let x = 0; x < N; x++) grid[y][x] = (Math.random() * COLORS) | 0; // 0..4 } canvas.addEventListener('mousedown', e => { const r = canvas.getBoundingClientRect(); const gx = ((e.clientX - r.left) / CELL) | 0; const gy = ((e.clientY - r.top) / CELL) | 0; select(gx, gy); });

Finding matches

Scan each row and column for three-or-more of the same value. Mark them, clear, then collapse cells downward and refill the top — the satisfying cascade.

match.js
function findMatches() { const dead = []; for (let y = 0; y < N; y++) for (let x = 0; x < N-2; x++) { const v = grid[y][x]; if (v === grid[y][x+1] && v === grid[y][x+2]) dead.push([y,x],[y,x+1],[y,x+2]); } // repeat vertically with grid[y+1][x], grid[y+2][x] return dead; }

Clear the dead cells (set to -1), then for each column pull surviving gems down and drop fresh random gems in from the top. Play a beep on each clear for instant juice.

🎮 Build This: Puzzle — Gem Match

Genre: Match-3 puzzle (NEW genre). This STARTS a new genre with no physics; it reuses the click-to-grid math and adds the new state machine and Web Audio.

  1. Build an 8x8 grid of random color indices; draw each cell as a colored square.
  2. Click to select a gem, click an adjacent one to swap them.
  3. After a swap, find all horizontal AND vertical runs of 3+; clear them.
  4. Collapse columns down and refill from the top; loop until no matches remain. Add score per gem cleared and a beep on each clear.
  5. Wrap it in a menu / play / game-over state machine. Bonus: undo a swap that makes no match.

title: Polish, Package & Deploy Your Capstone
html:
From project to portfolio

You have built a toy, two arcade games, a tapper, a platformer, a shooter, and a puzzle — across SIX genres, all from scratch. The final step is the one most learners skip: polish a game and ship it to the public web so you can put a link on your resume. This lesson's capstone is a portfolio-ready arcade game, deployed.

Choosing & scoping the capstone

Pick the game you enjoyed most and make it finished, not bigger. A small, polished game beats an ambitious unfinished one every time. A great target: a single-screen arcade game (an Asteroids-style blaster, an enhanced Breakout, or a wave-survival shooter) with a menu, score, sound, and a game-over loop.

Polish that players notice

  • Juice — screen shake on impact, a brief hit-flash, particles when something dies.
  • Feedback — sound on every meaningful action (reuse last lesson's beep).
  • Flow — a clean menu, a pause key, a restart without reloading the page.
  • Persistence — save the high score with localStorage.
polish.js
// high score that survives reloads let best = +(localStorage.getItem('best') || 0); function setScore(s) { if (s > best) { best = s; localStorage.setItem('best', best); } } // quick screen shake let shake = 0; function render() { ctx.save(); if (shake > 0) { ctx.translate((Math.random()-.5)*shake, (Math.random()-.5)*shake); shake -= 0.5; } drawWorld(); ctx.restore(); } // on a big hit: shake = 8;

A tiny particle burst

Particles are just short-lived objects with velocity and a fading life. Spawn a handful on each kill; the effect is dramatic for almost no code.

particles.js
const parts = []; function burst(x, y) { for (let i = 0; i < 12; i++) { const a = Math.random() * Math.PI * 2; parts.push({ x, y, vx: Math.cos(a)*120, vy: Math.sin(a)*120, life: 0.5 }); } } function updateParts(dt) { for (const p of parts) { p.x += p.vx*dt; p.y += p.vy*dt; p.life -= dt; } for (let i = parts.length-1; i >= 0; i--) if (parts[i].life <= 0) parts.splice(i,1); }

Make it fit any screen

Set the canvas internal resolution in code and scale it with CSS so it fills the window while keeping aspect ratio. Read input through getBoundingClientRect (you already do) and it just works on phones too.

Tip.
Keep your whole capstone in ONE index.html. A single self-contained file with no build step is the easiest possible thing to host — and impossible to break in deployment.

Deploying to the public web

A canvas game is static files, so hosting is free and instant. Three solid options:

HostHowBest for
itch.ioZip your files (index.html at the root), create a project, upload, mark "playable in browser"Game portfolios, a real game page
GitHub PagesPush to a repo, enable Pages on the main branchFree, version-controlled, custom domain
Netlify / Cloudflare PagesDrag the folder into the dashboard, or connect the repoOne-click deploys, instant URLs
deploy.sh
# GitHub Pages in a few commands git init git add . && git commit -m 'My capstone game' git branch -M main # create the repo on github, then: git remote add origin <your-repo-url> && git push -u origin main # Settings -> Pages -> Source: main / root -> Save
Deploy gotcha.
Hosts are case-sensitive and serve from the repo root. If your file is Ship.png but your code says ship.png, it works on a Mac but breaks live. Match case exactly, and keep index.html at the top level of what you upload.
🎮 Build This: Capstone — Polished Arcade Game, DEPLOYED

Genre: Polished arcade (your choice). This is the CAPSTONE — extend one of your earlier games (Breakout, the shooter, or a fresh Asteroids-style blaster) into a finished, portfolio-ready title and ship it live.

  1. Pick a game and wrap it in a full state machine: menu, play, pause, game over.
  2. Add sound on every key action and a localStorage high score.
  3. Add juice: screen shake, a hit-flash, and particle bursts on kills.
  4. Make the canvas scale to the window so it plays on desktop and mobile.
  5. Deploy it to itch.io, GitHub Pages, or Cloudflare Pages, and confirm the live URL plays in a fresh browser. Put that link in your portfolio — you are now a game developer.
